title: קְדוּשְׁתָּאוֹת לְשַׁבָּת הַגָּדוֹל | Qedushtaot cycle and "seder" azhara for Shabbat haGadol, by Rabbi Yosef Bonfils (and Yannai)
author: Isaac Gantwerk Mayer (transcription & naqdanut)

coauthors.name: Isaac Gantwerk Mayer (transcription & naqdanut)
coauthors.bio: From a family of musicians, Isaac Gantwerk Mayer believes that creative art is one of the most powerful ways to get in touch with the divine. He composes music and poetry in Hebrew and English. (He also translates and authors his own original works.) Isaac runs a Jewish music transcription service, which will transcribe and set any Jewish music in any language, recorded or written. Contact his service on Facebook or via his music blog.

coauthors.name: Joseph ben Samuel Bonfils
coauthors.bio: Joseph ben Samuel Bonfils (fl. middle of the 11th c.) was a French rabbi, Talmudist, Bible commentator, and payyeṭan. He is also known by the Hebrew name Tov Elem, a Hebrew translation from the French name "Bonfils." Of his life nothing is known but that he came from Narbonne, and was rabbi of Limoges in the province of Anjou. The ability and activity of Bonfils are best judged from his contributions to the poetry of the synagogue, no less than sixty-two of his piyyuṭim occupying prominent places in the French, German, and Polish liturgies. (Joseph Bonfils must not be confused, as he is by Azulai, with another scholar of the same name, who lived in 1200 and corresponded with Simḥah of Speyer (Responsa of Meïr ben Baruch of Rothenburg. ed. Cremona, No. 148).)

coauthors.name: Yanai haPayetan
coauthors.bio: Yannai (יניי or ינאי‎) was an important payyetan who lived in the late fifth-early sixth century in the Galilee in Byzantine Palestine. Sometimes referred to as the "father of piyyut," his poetry marks the beginning of the Classical Period of piyyut that ranged from the fifth-eighth centuries. He was the first poet of piyyut to sign his name in an acrostic, to use end-rhyme, and to wrote for weekly services (not just for the High Holidays and particular festivals). According to Laura Lieber, the liturgical form most associated with Yannai is the qedushta, which embellishes the first 3 blessing of the Amidah (a part of the Jewish prayer service).

excerpt: The full qedushtaot and "seder" azhara for Shabbat haGadol, largely by the great eleventh-century paytan R. Yosef Bonfils (with one additional rahit by Yannai), including all the catenae of verses and repeated refrains often skipped in Ashkenazi practice. Alphabetical texts are translated with alphabetical acrostics.
content:
The full qedushtaot and “seder” azhara for Shabbat haGadol, largely by the great eleventh-century paytan R. Yosef Bonfils (with one additional rahit by Yannai), including all the catenae of verses and repeated refrains often skipped in Ashkenazi practice. Alphabetical texts are translated with alphabetical acrostics.
Source (Hebrew) Translation (English)
בָּרוּךְ אַתָּה
יְהֹוָה אֱלֹהֵֽינוּ וֵאלֹהֵי אֲבוֹתֵֽינוּ׃
אֱלֹהֵי אַבְרָהָם אֱלֹהֵי יִצְחָק וֵאלֹהֵי יַעֲקֹב׃
הָאֵל
הַגָּדוֹל הַגִּבּוֹר וְהַנּוֹרָא
אֵל עֶלְיוֹן׃
גּוֹמֵל חֲסָדִים טוֹבִים
וְקוֹנֵה הַכֹּל
וְזוֹכֵר חַסְדֵי אָבוֹת
וּמֵבִיא גוֹאֵל לִבְנֵי בְנֵיהֶם
לְמַֽעַן שְׁמוֹ
בְּאַהֲבָה׃
מֶֽלֶךְ עוֹזֵר
וּמוֹשִֽׁיעַ וּמָגֵן׃
Blessed are You,
o Cause, our God and God of our ancestors.
God of Abraham, God of Isaac, and God of Jacob.
The Deity,
great and mighty and awesome;
the almighty Deity,
who well repays kindness,
and possesses all,
and remembers the kindnesses of ancestors
and sends a redeemer to their descendants
for Their Name’s sake,
in love.
Sovereign, Help
and Rescue and Shield —
רשות
Reshut[1] This brief reshut is inserted into the Ashkenazi rite here to justify the practice of inserting piyyutim. 
מִסּוֹד חֲכָמִים וּנְבוֹנִים
וּמִלֶּֽמֶד דַּֽעַת מְבִינִים
אֶפְתְּחָה פִּי בְּשִׁיר וּרְנָנִים
לְהוֹדוֹת וּלְהַלֵּל פְּנֵי שׁוֹכֵן מְעוֹנִים׃
By the counsel of sages and scholars,
and the study of knowledge-understanders,
let me open my mouth in song and cheer
to thank and praise the countenance of the Dweller on High.
מגן
Magen
אֱלֹהִים בְּצַעְדְּךָ הַכּוֹת פַּתְרוֹס
בִּתַּֽקְתָּ חֵילָם מִבְצְרֵיהֶם לַהֲרֹס
גָּאַֽלְתָּ עַמְּךָ בְּנֵֽבֶל קַתְרוֹס
דְּרַרְתָּם וְהִבְרַרְתָּם כְּסֹֽלֶת לָרֹס׃
As You, God stepped out to strike Egypt’s southland,[2] In the Hebrew, Patros From the Egyptian pꜣ-tꜣ-rsj (the land of the south), this term referred to Upper Egypt, the area of the Nile Valley south of the delta. 
Breaking into their troop, their fortresses to disband,
Cittern and lyre as You redeemed Your people’s band,
Delivering them, sifting like semolina mixed by hand.
הִגַּֽרְתָּ לַגַּי הֲמוֹן פִּיבֶֽסֶת
וְנִקָּֽתָה לָאָֽרֶץ בִּטְרִיָּה נֶחְבֶּֽסֶת
זְנוּחָה זָרְחָה בְּדַם מִתְבּוֹסֶֽסֶת
חֻלָּצָה וְנִגְהָצָה בִּרְחִיצָה וְתִכְבֹּֽסֶת׃
Exiling to the valley the horde of Bubastis,[3] In the Hebrew, Pi-beset. Find Ezekiel 30:17 
Floundering on the ground, borne down and abscessed.
Glowing, the once-forsaken, blood-coated, oppressed,
Had strengthened in bathing and in washing refreshed.
טֶֽרֶם מֵרוּר מִמְּעֻנִּים יֻשְׁבַּת
יְשׁוּעָה צָמְחָה לִנְצוּרֵי כְּבָבַת
כִּתַּֽרְתָּ כְלִיל לְנֶֽפֶשׁ מְשִׁיבַת
לְשֶׁעָבַר קִדַּֽמְתָּ מַתַּן שַׁבָּת׃
In advance of the bitter yokedness’ removal,
Jumped salvation for those guarded like a pupil.
Kitting out with a crown of soul-refreshing approval,
Letting them know first of the sabbath’s behooval.
In most modern Ashkenazi practice, the catenae of verses inserted after the first three piyyutim are not recited.
כַּכָּתוּב׃
רְא֗וּ כִּֽי־יְהֹוָה֮ נָתַ֣ן לָכֶ֣ם הַשַּׁבָּת֒
עַל־כֵּ֠ן ה֣וּא נֹתֵ֥ן לָכֶ֛ם בַּיּ֥וֹם הַשִּׁשִּׁ֖י לֶ֣חֶם יוֹמָ֑יִם
שְׁב֣וּ ׀ אִ֣ישׁ תַּחְתָּ֗יו
אַל־יֵ֥צֵא אִ֛ישׁ מִמְּקֹמ֖וֹ
בַּיּ֥וֹם הַשְּׁבִיעִֽי׃ (שמות טז:כט)
As it is written:
See that the Cause gave you the sabbath,
thus They are giving you on the sixth day two days’ bread;
each man stay where he is,
let no man go out from his place
on the seventh day. (Exodus 16:29)
וְנֶאֱמַר׃
וָאֶרְחָצֵ֣ךְ בַּמַּ֔יִם
וָאֶשְׁטֹ֥ף דָּמַ֖יִךְ מֵעָלָ֑יִךְ
וָאֲסֻכֵ֖ךְ בַּשָּֽׁמֶן׃ (יחזקאל טז:ט)
And it is said:
And I washed you in water
and I removed the blood from upon you
and I rubbed you with oil. (Ezekiel 16:9)
וְנֶאֱמַר׃
כִּ֤י כָאָ֙רֶץ֙ תּוֹצִ֣יא צִמְחָ֔הּ
וּכְגַנָּ֖ה זֵרוּעֶ֣יהָ תַצְמִ֑יחַ
כֵּ֣ן ׀ אֲדֹנָ֣י יֱהֹוִ֗ה
יַצְמִ֤יחַ צְדָקָה֙ וּתְהִלָּ֔ה
נֶ֖גֶד כׇּל־הַגּוֹיִֽם׃ (ישעיה סא:יא)
And it is said:
For as the land puts out its sprouts,
and a garden germinates its seeds,
thus the Master Cause
will germinate justice and praise
before all the nations. (Isaiah 61:11)
וְנֶאֱמַר׃
גָּאַ֣לְתָּ בִּזְר֣וֹעַ עַמֶּ֑ךָ
בְּנֵֽי־יַעֲקֹ֖ב וְיוֹסֵ֣ף
סֶֽלָה׃ (תהלים עז:טז)
And it is said:
You saved with Your right hand Your people,
the chilren of Jacob and Joseph,
forever. (Psalms 77:16)[4] The last word ‘selah’ was originally a musical instruction, but in rabbinic interpretation it refers to eternity. 
סֶֽלָה אֲרוֹמִמְךָ מוֹשִֽׁיעַ וְשַׁלִּיט
מוֹצִיאִי לַחׇפְשִׁי מִבֵּית מַעֲלִיט ^nbsp;
סַכּֽוֹתָ לְרֹאשִׁי לְהַשְׁאִיר פָּלִיט ^nbsp;
גָּנוֹן וְהִצִיל פָּסֹֽחַ וְהִמְלִיט׃ ^nbsp;
Forever may I exalt the Ruler and Securer,
Navigating me to liberty from the house of the obscurer.
Occulting my head, for the remnant to ensure her,
Protector and shield, bypasser and abjurer.
בָּרוּךְ אַתָּה יְהֹוָה מָגֵן אַבְרָהָם׃
Blessed are You, o Cause, shield of Abraham.
אַתָּה גִּבּוֹר לְעוֹלָם אֲדֹנָי
מְחַיֵּה מֵתִים אַתָּה רַב לְהוֹשִֽׁיעַ׃
מַשִּׁיב הָרֽוּחַ
וּמוֹרִיד הַגָּֽשֶׁם׃
מְכַלְכֵּל חַיִּים בְּחֶֽסֶד
מְחַיֵּה מֵתִים בְּרַחֲמִים רַבִּים׃
סוֹמֵךְ נוֹפְלִים
וְרוֹפֵא חוֹלִים
וּמַתִּיר אֲסוּרִים
וּמְקַיֵּם אֱמוּנָתוֹ לִישֵׁנֵי עָפָר׃
מִי כָמֽוֹךָ בַּֽעַל גְּבוּרוֹת
וּמִי דוֹמֶה לָּךְ׃
מֶֽלֶךְ מֵמִית וּמְחַיֶּה
וּמַצְמִֽיחַ יְשׁוּעָה׃
וְנֶאֱמָן אַתָּה לְהַחֲיוֹת מֵתִים —
You are mighty forever, Master,
You bring life to the dead, greatly saving.
You turn the wind
and send down the rain.
You provide for life in kindness,
reviving the dead in great mercy,
lift the fallen,
heal the sick,
free the bound,
and keep faith with sleepers in the dust.
Who is like You, Master of Powerful Acts,
and who can be compared to You?
Sovereign, who brings death and life
and causes salvation to sprout.
And trusted are You to revive the dead —
מחייה
Meḥaiyeh
מִמַּסְגֵּר אַסִּיר בְּצֵאת לְחֹֽפֶשׁ
נִתְנוֹסֵס נֵס בִּטְבִיעַת רֶֽפֶשׁ
סִגַּבְתָּם חֹק מַרְגּֽוֹעַ וָנֹֽפֶשׁ
עֲרֹךְ תְּחִלָּה לִמְשִׁיבַת נָֽפֶשׁ׃
Quarantined prisoners, when they went out free,
Quagmire-drowned ones raising a miraculous burgee,
Rest and refreshment You reinforced them by decree,
Ratified before the soul’s remedy.[5] Meaning the Torah. Find Psalms 19:8 
פֵּרוּשׁוֹ וְטַעֲמוֹ צִוִּֽיתָ בְמָרָה
צִחֲצֽוּחַ דָּתוֹ לְהָבִין וּלְגוֹמְרָה
קְבִיעַת קְדֻשָּׁתוֹ בִּזְכִירָה וּשְׁמִירָה
רֹן לְהָפִיק בְּתוֹדָה וְזִמְרָה׃
Signification and meaning You at Marah did ordain,
So as to understand completely its law pure and plain,
To set its sanctity by remembring and keeping twain,
To express cheer in thanksgiving and hymnal refrain.
שְׁפָטִים גְּדוֹלִים עָשִֽׂיתָ בְצֽוֹעַן
שִׁפְרַֽרְתָּ אֹֽהֶל בַּל־יִצְעָן
תְּעוּבִים פַּסּוּ בְּאַף לְהַכְנִיעָן
תָּקְפָה חֲרָדָה בֶּאֱדוֹם וּכְנָֽעַן׃
Upon Tanis[6] In Hebrew, Tso’an. An important city in New-Kingdom Egypt, often used as a synechdoche for the entire nation.  You did judgments great,
Unfurling a tent never to vacate.
Vanished are the foul ones, in rage to subjugate,
Vertigo seized Edom[7] Note that in most Ashkenazi practice today this word is read עֲמָלֵק “in Amaleq,” almost certainly a censorship on behalf of a church well aware of Jewish usage of “Edom” as a euphemism. The next verse in the catena mentions Edom directly, but, of course, in modern Ashkenazi practice the catenae are skipped so the censorship is less obvious.  and Canaan to agitate.
כַּכָּתוּב׃
אָ֤ז נִבְהֲלוּ֙ אַלּוּפֵ֣י אֱד֔וֹם
אֵילֵ֣י מוֹאָ֔ב יֹֽאחֲזֵ֖מוֹ רָ֑עַד
נָמֹ֕גוּ כֹּ֖ל יֹשְׁבֵ֥י כְנָֽעַן׃ (שמות טו:טו)
As it is written:
Thus the chiliarchs of Edom were panicked,
the chieftains of Moab seized by trembling,
stunned were all the inhabitants of Canaan. (Exodus 15:15)
וְנֶאֱמַר׃
וְאַתָּ֞ה דַּבֵּ֨ר אֶל־בְּנֵ֤י יִשְׂרָאֵל֙ לֵאמֹ֔ר
אַ֥ךְ אֶת־שַׁבְּתֹתַ֖י תִּשְׁמֹ֑רוּ
כִּי֩ א֨וֹת הִ֜וא בֵּינִ֤י וּבֵֽינֵיכֶם֙ לְדֹרֹ֣תֵיכֶ֔ם
לָדַ֕עַת כִּ֛י אֲנִ֥י יְהֹוָ֖ה מְקַדִּשְׁכֶֽם׃ (שמות לא:יג)
And it is said:
And you, speak to the children of Israel to say:
yet My sabbaths you will keep,
for it is a sign between Me and you for all ages,
to know that I the Cause santify you. (Exodus 31:13)[8] Found in Parma 3136 but not Berlin 1224. 
וְנֶאֱמַר׃
אִם־תָּשִׁ֤יב מִשַּׁבָּת֙ רַגְלֶ֔ךָ
עֲשׂ֥וֹת חֲפָצֶ֖ךָ בְּי֣וֹם קׇדְשִׁ֑י
וְקָרָ֨אתָ לַשַּׁבָּ֜ת עֹ֗נֶג
לִקְד֤וֹשׁ יְהֹוָה֙ מְכֻבָּ֔ד
וְכִבַּדְתּוֹ֙ מֵעֲשׂ֣וֹת דְּרָכֶ֔יךָ
מִמְּצ֥וֹא חֶפְצְךָ֖
וְדַבֵּ֥ר דָּבָֽר׃ (ישעיה נח:יג)
And it is said:
If you hold back your foot on the sabbath,
from doing your desires on My holy day,
and call the sabbath a delight,
the Cause’s holiness honored,
and honor it over going your ways,
finding your desires,
and speaking matters… (Isaiah 58:13)[9] Berlin 1224 includes an additional “and it is said” halfway through this verse, and continues with Isaiah 58:14.This is likely a scribal error since the next catena begins with it. 
וְנֶאֱמַר׃
וְהִתְעַנַּ֥ג עַל־יְהֹוָ֑ה
וְיִֽתֶּן־לְ֝ךָ֗ מִשְׁאֲלֹ֥ת לִבֶּֽךָ׃ (תהלים לז:ד)
And it is said:
And be delighted upon the Cause,
and They will give you the desires of your heart. (Psalms 37:4)
לִבֶּֽךָ וְעֵינֶֽיךָ לְהֵיכָלְךָ תְּשׁוֹבֵב
דָּשֵׁן וְשָׁמֵן לְעַמְּךָ יְנוֹבֵב ^nbsp;
תְּכוֹנֵן אֻלָמְךָ מִזְבֵּֽחַ וְסוֹבֵב ^nbsp;
לְהַגְשִׁים תֶּֽחִי יְשֵׁנִים דוֹבֵב׃ ^nbsp;
Your heart with Your eyes to Your palace return,[10] In modern Ashkenazi practice, as the catenae are skipped, this word is vocalized with the non-pausa form לִבְּךָ, as would be expected if it were not a direct citation of the last word of Psalms 37:4. 
Extruding rich oil for Your people to earn.
Your temple and altar establish in turn,
Zephyring revival that the sleepers may discern.
בָּרוּךְ אַתָּה יְהֹוָה מְחַיֵּה הַמֵּתִים׃
Blessed are You, o Cause, who revives the dead.
משלש
Meshalesh
יִשְׁעִי וּכְבוֹדִי מִשְׂגַּבִּי וּמְנוּסִי
וְנִצָּב לָרִיב עַל־אוֹנְסִי
סוֹכְכִי בְּאֶבְרָתוֹ יְהֹוָה נִסִּי
פּוֹרֵֽעַ וְגוֹבֶה דִין קְנָסִי׃
Yea, my Savior and Glory, my Refuge and Haven,
Opposing my oppressors, rises up, throws a glaive in;
Shading me with Their wings – the Cause’s flag I wave in!
Exacting and requiting my judgment on the craven.
בְּהִלּוֹ נֵרוֹ עֲלֵי רֹאשִׁי
רָכַב עַל־עָב קַל מֵעִנּוּי לְגׇרְשִׁי

מְקוֹם צִפְעוֹנִים נָחָשׁ שָׂרָף וְעַקְרָב
נָסְעוּ קְדוֹשִׁים לַשֶּֽׁמֶשׁ וְשָׁרָב
שָׂב וְעוֹלֵל וּמִקְנֶה רָב׃
יׄוֹׄצְאֵי חִפָּזוֹן סִׄפְּׄקָם מָזוֹן לְשׂוֹבַע וְלֹא לְרָזוֹן׃
קָדוֹשׁ
Nests of vipers, cobra and scorpion and snake,
Over them the holy passed, while the hot sun did bake;
Puerile and elderly, and much cattle in their wake.
Going out in speed, You provided feed, for satiety, not need;
holy!
עֲדָרִים הִשְׂכִּֽילוּ מַדָּע לְהַחֲכִּימָם
פִּסַּת מִשְׁאָרוֹת נָשְׂאוּ בְּהַשְׁכִּימָם
צְרֻרֹ֥ת בְּשִׂמְלֹתָ֖ם עַל־שִׁכְמָֽם׃
יׄוֹׄצְאֵי חִפָּזוֹן סִׄפְּׄקָם מָזוֹן לְשׂוֹבַע וְלֹא לְרָזוֹן׃
קָדוֹשׁ
Quick-wittedly, the flocks had knowledge to gain,
Rising still were the pieces they took from the grain,
Shoulder-borne, tied up in their cloaks[24] Exodus 12:34  like a chain.
Going out in speed, You provided feed, for satiety, not need;
holy!
קִטּוּף הָעִסָּה בַּחֲרָרָה קְמוּצָה
רְצוּפָה בְּרָאשֵׁיהֶם שֶׁלֹּא חֻמָּצָה
שֶֽׁמֶשׁ שֶׁזָפַתָּהּ וַאֲכָלֽוּהָ מַצָּה׃
יׄוֹׄצְאֵי חִפָּזוֹן סִׄפְּׄקָם מָזוֹן לְשׂוֹבַע וְלֹא לְרָזוֹן׃
קָדוֹשׁ
Taking bits of dough, unleavened cakes they made,
Vigorously pressed on their heads; whereby fermenting be stayed,
Exsiccated by yonder sun, on zyme-free bread they buffeted!
Going out in speed, You provided feed, for satiety, not need;
holy!
תּוּפִינֵי שִׁשִּׁים וְאַחַת בְּמִשְׁטָר
טָׄעֲמוּ וְׄאָרְחוּ בְּׄשִׁיּוּר הַמְנֻטָּר
עַׄד לֶֽׄחֶם מִׄשָּׁמַֽיִם הֻמְטָר׃
יׄוֹׄצְאֵי חִפָּזוֹן סִׄפְּׄקָם מָזוֹן לְשׂוֹבַע וְלֹא לְרָזוֹן׃
קָדוֹשׁ
Sixty-one baked meals[25] To cover from the exodus to the sixteenth of Iyyar, when the manna began to fall  ahead of time they prepared,
Breaking off nuggets from which they ate, kept, and shared,
In the end, lofty bread soared down on which they fared.
Going out in speed, You provided feed, for satiety, not need;
holy!
רהיט
Rahit[26] Note that unlike the rest of this cycle, this rahit was written by Yannai. It is one of two portions of the Shabbat haGadol liturgical cycle which have, in Ashkenazi practice, been appended to the end of the Seder night. This translation was originally published as part of my haggadah last year. 
וּבְכֵן וַיְהִ֣י ׀ בַּחֲצִ֣י הַלַּ֗יְלָה (שמות יב:כט) —
And thus… it was in the middle of the night. (Exodus 12:29)
אָז רוֹב נִסִּים הִפְלֵֽאתָ בַּלַּֽיְלָה
בְּרֹאשׁ אַשְׁמֽוֹרֶת זֶה הַלַּֽיְלָה
גֵר צֶֽדֶק נִצַּחְתּוֹ כְּנֶחֱלַק לוֹ לַֽיְלָה
וַיְהִ֣י ׀ בַּחֲצִ֣י הַלַּ֗יְלָה
Most wondrous miracles You did at night.
At first watch, this very night.
You made the righteous convert[27] Abraham. Find Genesis 15 and the “covenant between the pieces.”  victorious night.
when it was divided for him at
And it was in the middle of the night.
דַּנְתָּ מֶֽלֶךְ גְּרָר בַּחֲלוֹם הַלַּֽיְלָה
הִפְחַֽדְתָּ אֲרַמִי בְּאֶֽמֶשׁ לַֽיְלָה
וַיָּֽשַׂר יִשְׂרָאֵל לְמַלְאָךְ וַיוּכַל לוֹ לַֽיְלָה
וַיְהִ֣י ׀ בַּחֲצִ֣י הַלַּ֗יְלָה
You judged Gerar’s king[28] Abimelekh. Find Genesis 20:3  in dream of night.
You frightened the Aramean[29] Laban. Find Genesis 31:24  in dark night.
Israel wrestled[30] Find Genesis 32:25–31  and withstood an angel at night.
And it was in the middle of the night.
זֶֽרַע בְּכוֹרֵי פַתְרוֹס מָחַֽצְתָּ בַּחֲצִי הַלַּֽיְלָה
חֵילָם לֹא־מָצְאוּ בְּקוּמָם בַּלַּֽיְלָה
טִיסַת נְגִיד חֲרֹֽשֶׁת סִלִֽיתָ בְּכוֹכְבֵי לַֽיְלָה
וַיְהִ֣י ׀ בַּחֲצִ֣י הַלַּ֗יְלָה
The firstborn seed of Upper Egypt You crushed at midnight.
Not finding their wealth when they rose at night.
The attack of Ḥaróshet’s leader[31] Sisera. Find Judges 5:20.  You swept by stars of night.
And it was in the middle of the night.
יָֽעַץ מְחָרֵף לְנוֹפֵף אִוּוּי הוֹבַֽשְׁתָּ פְגָרָיו בַּלַּֽיְלָה
כָּרַע בֵּל וּמַצָבוֹ בְּאִישׁוֹן לַֽיְלָה
לְאִישׁ חֲמוּדוֹת נִגְלָה רָז חֲזוֹת לַֽיְלָה
וַיְהִ֣י ׀ בַּחֲצִ֣י הַלַּ֗יְלָה
The blasphemer[32] Sennacherib. Find Kings 2:19:35.  planned to wave the desired;
You dressed him in corpses at night.
Bel and his pedestal were bent in pitch night.
The delightful man[33] Referring to Daniel. Find Daniel 7 for his night vision, and Daniel 10 for his moniker “delightful man.”  was shown secret visions of night.
And it was in the middle of the night.
מִשְׁתַּכֵּר בִּכְלֵי קֹֽדֶשׁ נֶהֱרַג בּוֹ בַּלַּֽיְלָה
נוֹשַׁע מִבּוֹר אֲרָיוֹת פּוֹתֵר בִּעֲתוּתֵי לַֽיְלָה
שִׂנְאָה נָטַר אֲגָגִי וְכָתַב סְפָרִים בַּלַּֽיְלָה
וַיְהִ֣י ׀ בַּחֲצִ֣י הַלַּ֗יְלָה
The drinker from holy vessels[34] Belshazzar — find Daniel 5:30  was killed by night.
The one saved from the lions’s den[35] Daniel again. The lion’s den is in Daniel 6. He interprets nightmares in chapter 2. 
explained the mares-of-night.
The Agagite[36] Haman. Find Esther 3:1  kept hatred and wrote books by night.
And it was in the middle of the night.
עוֹרַרְתָּ נִצְחֲךָ עָלָיו בְּנֶֽדֶד שְׁנַת לַֽיְלָה
פּוּרָה תִדְרוֹךְ לשֹׁמֵר֙ מַה־מִּלַּ֔יְלָה (ישעיה כא:יא)
צָרַח כַּשׁוֹמֵר וְשָׂח אָתָ֥ה בֹ֖קֶר וְגַם־לָ֑יְלָה (ישעיה כא:יב)
וַיְהִ֣י ׀ בַּחֲצִ֣י הַלַּ֗יְלָה
You roused victory on him[37] Of Aḥashverosh. Find Esther 6:1  by disturbing sleep at night.
You will stomp the press for the “watchman, what of night?” (Isaiah 21:11)
Yelling like a guard, saying, “morning has come, and also night!” (Isaiah 21:12)
And it was in the middle of the night.
קָרֵב יוֹם אֲשֶׁר הוּא לֹא־יוֹם וְלֹא־לַֽיְלָה
רָם הוֹדַע כִּי־לְךָ הַיוֹם אַף־לְךָ הַלַּֽיְלָה
שׁוֹמְרִים הַפְקֵד לְעִירְךָ כׇּל־הַיוֹם וְכׇל־הַלַּֽיְלָה
תָּאִיר כְּאוֹר יוֹם חֶשְׁכַּת לַֽיְלָה
וַיְהִ֣י ׀ בַּחֲצִ֣י הַלַּ֗יְלָה
Bring close the day neither day nor night![38] Zechariah 14:7 
High One, show that Yours is both day and night!
Appoint guards for Your city all day and all night!
Illuminate like daylight the darkness of night!
And it was in the middle of the night.
רשות
Reshut[39] Another reshut, this one asking permission to insert the seder (which follows immediately afterward). 
וּבְכֵן… כׇּל־מַחְמֶ֖צֶת לֹ֣א תֹאכֵ֑לוּ (שמות יב:כ)
And thus — all leaven do not eat. (Exodus 12:20)
אָבֹא בְחִיל לְהִתְיַצְּבָה
בְּמַעֲמַד פְּנֵי תֵבָה
גַּשְׁתִּי לְרוֹמֵם וּלְשַׂגְּבָה
מֵרְשׁוּת דָּגוּל מֵרְבָבָה׃
Anxiously I come to stand
Before the holy ark at my post, as planned
Getting close, to cheer and declare grand
By permission of the Deity bannered over myriads grand.
הִלּוּל קוֹנִי אַקְשִֽׁיבָה
וּבְחֵֽקֶר דָּתוֹ אֶתְיַשְּׁבָה
זֹאת לָדַֽעַת אֲחַשְּׁבָה
מִטַּֽעַם חַכְמֵי יְשִׁיבָה׃
Exultation of my Acquirer, I should make heard;
While I focus on investigating Their lawful word,
Zealously I plan, to knowledge spurred;
By interpretation of the honored sages of the seated herd.
טוֹעֵן עוֹלָם אַמְלִֽיכָה
יְהָבִי עָלָיו אַשְׁלִֽיכָה
כְּרָשׁ לְפָנָיו אֶתְהַלְּכָה
לִפְתֹּֽחַ בִּדְבַר הֲלָכָה׃
To enthrone the Upholder of existence,
Jettisoning upon Them my burdensome resistance,
Contritely before Them I will walk a distance,
Let me open in halakhic talk with consistence.
מִלַּת פִּי גְּדוּשָׁה
נֹצֵר חֶֽסֶד אַקְדִּֽישָׁה
סַלְסֵל בְּדֵעָה חֲדָשָׁה
מֵרְשׁוּת עֵדָה הַקְּדוֹשָׁה׃
My mouth’s words are overflowing;
Now let me sanctify the Kind and Easygoing;
Sonorously renewing knowing,
By permission of the order, holiness showing.
פְּנֵי יְשָׁרִים דּוֹבֵר
צִחֲצֽוּחַ אֲמָרִים אֲגַבֵּר
קִדַֽמְתִּי לִטֹּל בְּסֵֽבֶר
רְשׁוּת מֵרַב וְחָבֵר׃
Present before the Speaker Upright
Championing words of pure light,
Quickly, let me ask — I’ll be polite —
Rabbi and comrade, permit this rite!
שַׁדַּי חֵילִי תְאַמֵּץ
תַּעְדִּיף קָט וְקֹֽמֶץ
לְבָאֵר בְּלִי שֶֽׁמֶץ
הִלְכוֹת בִּעוּר חָמֵץ׃
Shore up my strength, Surveyor in heaven,
That You favor my little handful, my steaven,
To clarify without fault
the laws of removing leaven.
סדר
Seder[40] The seder, or azharah, is a poetic halakhic discourse on the laws of Pesaḥ. The first half, an alphabetical acrostic, discusses the laws of searching for and disposing of leaven. The second half, a name-acrostic, discusses the laws of the seder night itself. As a halakhic discourse, it features a lot of Aramaic terminology, marked here in red. Because the point of the seder is the context rather than the poetic form, the translation does not preserve the rhyme scheme. However, the acrostic is preserved in order to help with following along. 
אֱלֹהֵי הָרוּחוֹת לְכׇל־בָּשָׂר
חֹק לְעַמּוֹ מָסָר
וְהִזְהִירָם בִּכְתָב מוּסָר
לִבְדֹּק חָמֵץ אוֹר לְאַרְבָּעָה עָשָׂר׃
As the God of the spirits of all flesh
delivered a law to Their people,
warning them in written rebuke
to search for leaven on the evening of the fourteenth,
בְּשָׁעָה שֶׁאֵין בְּנֵי־אָדָם מְצוּיִים בְּשֽׁוּקָא
אוֹר הַנֵּר יָפֶה לִבְדִיקָה
אֵין בּוֹדְקִין לֹא־לְאוֹר הַחַמָּה וְלֹא־לְאוֹר הַלְּבָנָה וְלֹא־לְאוֹר הָאֲבוּקָה
וְצָרִיךְ לְבָרֵךְ עַל־בִּעוּר חָמֵץ כְּחֻקָּה׃
by the time that people are no longer found in the market.
Candlelight is best for searching —
one may not search by sunlight or moonlight or torchlight[41] Pesaḥim 2a:1  —
and one must bless “on the removal of leaven” per the law.[42] Pesaḥim 7a:16 
גָּמַר מְנַטְּרֵהּ עַד־דְּבַתְרָא
וּמִיָּד צָרִיךְ לְבַטֵּל הַשְּׁאָר בַּאֲמִירָה
וְלֵימָא הָכִי כׇּל־חֲמִירָא דְּאִכָּא בַּהֲדָא דִירָה
דְּלָא־חֲמִתֵּהּ וּדְלָא־בִעַרְתֵּהּ לִבָּטֵל וְלֶהֱוֵי כְּעַפְרָא׃
Guard it carefully until the next day,
then immediately nullify the remainder with speech.[43] Pesaḥim 6b:12 
One should say thus: “All leaven that I have in my dwelling
that I have not seen or removed should be nullified, made like dust.”
דְּאִי מִשְׁתַּכַּח לְאַחַר אוֹתָהּ שָׁעָה
אֵינוֹ חַיָּב בְּבַל־יֵרָאֶה וּבְבַל־יִמָּצֵא לְפוֹשְׁעָה
וְהָרוֹצֶה אַחַר בְּדִיקָתוֹ לֶאֱכֹל חָמֵץ וּלְשׇׂבְעָה
מַה שֶׁמְּשַׁיֵּר יַנִּיחֶֽנּוּ בְּצִנְעָה׃
Delayedly if it is found,
one is not liable for “Let it not be seen nor found” as a sin.[44] Pesaḥim 6b:16 
One who wants, after his search, to eat leaven to satiety
must put away his leftovers in in hiddenness.[45] Pesaḥim 9b:7 
הֵיכָא דְבָדַק בְּחוֹרֵי בֵיתָא
וְהִנִּֽיחַ אַרְבַּע אוֹ־חָמֵשׁ רִפְתָּא
וְאַשְׁכַּח דְּחָדָא מִנַּֽיְהוּ פַּחֲתָא
לִבְדֹּק מֵרֵישָׁא שֶׁמָּא חוּלְדָא לְקָחַתָּהּ׃
Even if he examined all the nooks of the house,
leaving four or five loaves,
if he finds that one of them is missing
he must search again because a weasel might have taken it.[46] Pesaḥim 9b:8 
וְכֵן תָּנוּ רַבָּנָן בְּפִרְקִין
דְּחוֹרֵי בֵיתָא וּנְקִיקִין
עִלָּאֵי וְתַתָּאֵי אֵינָן נִבְדָּקִין
וְאֶמְצָעִים זְקוּקִין׃
While our rabbis taught in their chapters
that the nooks and crannies of a house,
upper and lower ones are not searched,
but the middle ones are liable.[47] Pesaḥim 8a:9 
זִהֲרוּ גַּג הַיָּצִֽיעַ וְגַג מִגְדָּלִין
וְרֶֽפֶת בָּקָר וּמַתְבֵּן וְלוּלִין
וְאוֹצְרוֹת יַֽיִן וְשֶֽׁמֶן הַנְּעוּלִין
לִבְדֹּק חָמֵץ בְּלִי־עוֹלִין׃
Zealously they explained: the sloped-roof of a veranda or cupboard,
a cowshed, a straw-barn, coops,
and locked storerooms of wine and oil,
do not mandate the search for leaven.[48] Pesaḥim 8a:6 
חֹר שֶׁבֵּין אָדָם לַחֲבֵרוֹ
זֶה וְזֶה בּוֹדֵק עַד־שֶׁמַּגִֽיעַ וּמְבַטֵּל שְׁאָרוֹ
וְכֵן בֵּין יְהוּדִי וְגוֹי חַיָּב לְבַעֲרוֹ
פְּלֵימוֹ מֵקַל שֶׁמָּא יֹאמַר הַגּוֹי כְּשָׁפוֹ וַחֲבָרוֹ׃
Hole in the wall between two people,

but if not, conditum[90] Yerushalmi Pesaḥim 10:1:11 says that conditum fulfills the obligation for wine at the seder night. What’s conditum? A variety of spiced and sweetened white wine that was beloved in ancient Rome. The Apicius, one of the best preserved Roman-era cookbooks, has a recipe. The Tasting History Youtube channel provides a translation here, also including a thankfully scaled-down version in case you aren’t making enough for an entire villa. I tried making conditum last year and it was surprisingly good! A very nice after-dinner apertif! If you do it yourself, definitely use long pepper — it adds a slight fruitiness that regular black pepper doesn’t have. And get good wildflower honey, something local. I’m not sure what the bay leaves did — maybe spikenard would have added more. I think it might work as a vermouth substitute in a mixed drink. Here’s a picture.  and cooked wine in its place.

In that location, the phrase “just as we merited to order it, so too may we merit to do it” is interpreted as “just as we merited to do the seder, so too may we merit to do the sacrifice.” However, in context, at the end of Bonfils’ seder poem, it means something very different: “just as we merited to recite the seder poem, so too may we merit to do the seder!”

לָֽמָּה פֶּֽסַח בָּא מִן־הַצֹּאן
שֶׁפָּסַח וְחָמַל עַל־קְרוּאֵי צֹאן
וּכְרֹעֶה מְקַבֵּץ טְלָאִים מְשָׁכָם וּגְאָלָם מִבַּעֲלֵי לָצוֹן׃
Why[149] The centerpiece of the silluq, the following passage goes through several aspects of the seder and gives interpretations and explanations, serving almost as answers to the notoriously unanswered four questions.  does the Pesaḥ-offering come from the flock?
For They passed over and spared those called “the flock,”
and like a shepherd gathering up lambs drew them out and redeemed them from the mocking masters.
וְלָֽמָּה לְקִיחָתוֹ קֹֽדֶם לִשְׁחִיטָתוֹ יָמִים אַרְבָּעָה
וְנִתַּן לְמוּלִים וְלַעֲרֵלִים נִפְסַל בְּהִיא הַשָּׁעָה
כְּדֵי שֶׁיִּהְיוּ בְּיָדָם מִצְוֺת מְצוּיוֹת לְהִשְׁתַּעֲשֵׁעָה
שֶׁכְּבָר בָּא עֵת דּוֹדִים וּזְמַן שְׁבוּעָה
וְהָיוּ מִתְבּוֹסְסִים בִּשְׁנֵי דָמִים שֶׁתַּצְמִֽיחַ יְשׁוּעָה׃
And why was it taken four days before it was slaughtered,
and given to the circumcised but void from the uncircumcised at that time?
So that they would have found in their hands commandments that may be delighting,
for already the era of loving and the time of the oath has come,
and they were winnowing in two bloods[150] According to Targum Yonatan on Ezekiel 16:6, the twofold repetition of “Live in your blood” in that verse — also the source for the imagery of wallowing, and found in the Haggadah — refers to the blood of the Pesaḥ sacrifice and the circumcision.  so that salvation would sprout.
וְלָֽמָּה נְתִינַת דָּמוֹ עַל־הַמַּשְׁקוֹף וּשְׁתֵּי הַמְּזוּזוֹת
שֶׁהֵן שָׁלֹשׁ מִזְבְּחוֹת בִּמְקוֹם רוֹאִים אוֹתָם לַחֲזוֹת
זֵֽכֶר שָׁלֹשׁ מִזְבְּחוֹת שֶׁל־אַב הֲמוֹן נִרְמָזוֹת
שֶׁבְּדָן וּבֵית־אֵל וְאֵלוֹן מוֹרֶה נֶחֱרָזוֹת׃
And why was its blood placed on the lintel and the two doorposts,
which are the three altars in visible places to be seen?
They are an allegorical memorial of the three altars that the father of multitudes built consecutively,
in Dan, Beth-el, and the terebinth of Moreh.[151] Abraham (the father of multitudes) builds three altars in Genesis 12:7, 12:8, and 13:18. Note, though, that none of them are built in Dan — they are built at Shechem, Bethel, and Moreh. Dan, however, is mentioned later in Abraham’s story, in the battle of the nine kings, in Genesis 14:14. 
וְלָֽמָּה אֲכִילַת חָמֵץ לְשֵׁשׁ שָׁעוֹת
זֵֽכֶר לְחִפְּזוֹן שְׁכִינָה לְהַעֲבִיר גְּזֵרוֹת רָעוֹת
מְדַלֵּג֙ עַל־הֶ֣הָרִ֔ים מְקַפֵּ֖ץ עַל־הַגְּבָעֽוֹת׃
And why may we eat leaven until the sixth hour?
As a memorial of the Presence’s speed in making wicked decrees pass away —
leaping over the mountains, bounding over the hills.[152] Song of Songs 2:9 
וְלָֽמָּה אֲכִילַת מַצָּה לֶֽחֶם עֹנִי
שֶׁלֹּא־הִסְפִּיק בָּצֵק לְהַחֲמִיץ עַד־שֶׁגְּאָלָם מֵעֹֽנִי׃
And why do we eat matzo, the poor-bread?
Because the dough did not have enough time to ferment before they were redeemed from poverty.
וְלָֽמָּה בְּמָרוֹר שֶׁתְּחִלָּתוֹ רַךְ וְסוֹפוֹ קָשֶׁה
שֶׁנִּמְרְרוּ תְּחִלָּה בְּפֶה רַךְ וְלִבְסוֹף בְּעֹל קָשֶׁה׃
And why with bitter-herbs, which start soft and end hard?[153] Referring to romaine, which is, in the wild, a biennial plant. In the first year its leaves are sweet and tender, and in the second year it grows a flower and its leaves wither and become bitter and tough. Pesaḥim 39a:10. 
Because they were bittered at first with a soft mouth,[154] A pun on the rare word ferekh (rigor) in the Exodus story, found first in Sotah 11b:1.  and at the end with a hard yoke.
וְלָֽמָּה טִבּוּלוֹ בַּחֲרֹֽסֶת
זֵֽכֶר לְטִיט שֶׁאִשָּׁה וּבַעְלָהּ דּוֹרֶֽסֶת׃
And why are they dipped in ḥaroset?
As a memorial of the mortar that husband and wife would tread.
וְלָֽמָּה תַּבְלִין בְּתוֹכוֹ מֻגְבָּלִין כְּמוֹ מַלְבֵּן
זֵֽכֶר לְנִפּוּץ עַם לְקֹשֵׁשׁ קַשׁ לַתֶּֽבֶן׃
And why are spices mixed into it, kneaded up like clay?
As a memorial of the people scattering to gather up stubble for straw.[155] Find Exodus 5:12. 
וְלָֽמָּה שְׁנֵי מִינֵי בָשָׂר בְּצַד עוּגָה
אֶחָד זֵֽכֶר לַפֶּֽסַח וְאֶחָד זֵֽכֶר לַחֲגִיגָה׃
And why are two kinds of meat are put beside the loaf?
One as a memorial of the Pesaḥ-offering, and one as a memorial of the festival-offering.
וְלָֽמָּה מַגְבִּיהִין קְעָרָה קֹֽדֶם זְמַנָּהּ
כְּדֵי שֶׁיִּרְאֶה תִּינוֹק וְיִשְׁאַל מַה־נִּשְׁתַּנָּה׃
And why is the plate removed before its time?
So that the child will see and ask “What makes this different?”[156] Pesaḥim 115b:9. In many practices, the plate is removed from the table entirely before the maggid. 
וְלָֽמָּה שְׁאָר יְרָקוֹת בָּאִין תְּחִלָּה
לִפְטֹר חֲזֶֽרֶת שֶׁל־מִצְוָה בְּרָכָה רְאוּיָה לָהּ
וְשֶׁאֵין לוֹמַר שְׁתֵּי בְרָכוֹת כְּאַחַת שְׁתִיָּה אוֹ־אֲכִילָה
וְאִי־אֶפְשָׁר לֶאֱכֹל וְלִשְׁתּוֹת כְּאַחַת בְּשֶׁכְּבָר כְּרֵסוֹ מִלָּא
וְכֵן בִּשְׁתֵּי מַצּוֹת פּוֹטְרִין מֵחַלָּה לְחַלָּה׃
עַל כִּי־אֵין לְשַׁנּוֹת בִּשְׁתֵּי בְרָכוֹת
מְבָרֵךְ עַל־זוֹ וְעַל־זוֹ וְאֵינוֹ אוֹכֵל עַד־שֶׁיִּגְמֹר שְׁתֵּי בְרָכוֹת׃
And why do other vegetables come first?
To exempt the commanded romaine from the blessing befitting it.[157] Normally one would say “Creator of the fruit of the earth” over the bitter-herbs, so we eat the other greens earlier so that we can say only the blessing of bitter-herbs themselves on them. 
and since we do not say two blessings on one beverage or food,
just as it is impossible to eat or drink when one has already filled his gut.
Thus two matzot exempt from loaf to loaf,
since we do not repeat with two blessings,
we bless on this one and on that one and do not eat until we have finished both blessings.[158] As in, we say the blessing of “who brings forth” on one and the blessing on matzo itself on the other loaf. 
וְלָֽמָּה אַרְבָּעָה כּוֹסוֹת מִצְוָתָן בְּזֶה הַלַּֽיְלָה
זֵֽכֶר לְאַרְבָּעָה גְּאֻלּוֹת שֶׁנִתְבַּשְּׂרוּ בָהֶם עַם סְגֻלָּה
יְצִיאָה וְהַצָּלָה לְקִיחָה וּגְאֻלָּה
וּלְהוֹדוֹת בָּהֶם וְלִתֵּן בָּם תְּהִלָּה
לְחוֹבֵט אַרְבָּעָה כוֹסוֹת שֶׁל־יְפֵה־פִיָּה עֶגְלָה
וּכְדֵי לְשַׁבֵּר אֻמּוֹת עֲמָמוֹת מְשֻׁקָּצוֹת בִּגְעִילָה
בְּאַרְבָּעָה כוֹסוֹת הַכְּתוּבִים בָּם שֶׁהֵן כּוֹס הַתַּרְעֵלָה
וּכְנֶגְדָּם לְהַשְׁקוֹתֵֽנוּ אַרְבָּעָה כוֹסוֹת שֶׁל־חֶמְלָה׃
And why are four cups commanded for this night?
As a memorial for the four redemptions that were heralded to the treasured people —
bringing and rescuing and taking and redeeming[159] The four verbs of action in Exodus 6:7. This and the following interpretations of the four cups are found in Yerushalmi Pesaḥim 10:1:7.  —
and to thank with them and give with them praise
to the One who struck the four cups[160] The four repetitions of “cup” in Genesis 40:11–13  of the handsome heifer,[161] Egypt. Find Jeremiah 46:20 
and to shatter the nations, the disgusting and foul peoples[162] This entire line, from “and to shatter” to here, is censored in many texts. In some “peoples” is replaced with ‘Amaleq. 
with four cups as written for them[163] In Jeremiah 23:15, Jeremiah 51:7, Psalms 75:9, and Psalms 11:6  which are the cup of wrath,
and corresponding to them to make us drink four cups of compassion.
וְלָֽמָּה מְסֻבִּין בְּמִטָּה כְבֻדָּה וּכְלוּלָה
זֵֽכֶר לְחֵרוּת וְרִבּוּץ עַנְנֵי כָבוֹד בַּחֲתֻלָּה
שֶׁכֵּן דֶּֽרֶךְ בְּנֵי מְלָכִים לִנְהֹג כָּבוֹד וּלְסַלְסְלָה
שֶׁעִטְּרָֽנוּ קְדוֹשׁ יַעֲקֹב עֲטֶֽרֶת מְלוּכָה וּתְהִלָּה
וּבֵרַר לְהוֹדִֽיעַ עַם מַרְעִיתוֹ לִהְיוֹת־לוֹ לְעַם נַחֲלָה
כִּ֣י לֵ֭אלֹהִים מָֽגִנֵּי־אֶ֗רֶץ מְאֹ֣ד נַעֲלָֽה׃ (תהלים מז:י)
And why do we recline on a gloriously decorated bed?
As a memorial of the freedom, and the massed clouds of glory in our enveloping.
For thus is the practice of princes to go about in glory and ostentation,
for Jacob’s Holy One crowned us with a crown of royalty and praise,
and clearly made known that Their pastured people will be Their inheritance-people,
for to God are the powers fo the world, greatly exalted. (Psalms 47:10)
נַעֲלָה בִקְדֻשָּׁתוֹ מָרוֹם וְקָדוֹשׁ
כִּי־אֱלֹהִים קְדוֹשִׁים הוּא בְּכׇל־מִינֵי קְדֻשָּׁה
דַרְכּוֹ בִּקְדֻשָּׁה
וְהִלּוּכוֹ בִּקְדֻשָּׁה
דִּבּוּרוֹ בִּקְדֻשָּׁה
יְשִׁיבָתוֹ בִּקְדֻשָּׁה
חֲשִׂיפַת זְרוֹעוֹ בִּקְדֻשָּׁה
נָאֶה וְאַדִּיר בִּקְדֻשָּׁה׃
Exalted in holiness is the Lofty and Holy,
for a holy[164] Conjugated in the plural, as sometimes occurs due to the pluralis majestatis nature of the Hebrew word “God.”  God are They in all kinds of holiness;
Their way is in holiness
and Their practice is in holiness;
Their word is in holiness,
Their seat is in holiness,
Their outstretched arm is in holiness,
beautiful and mighty in holiness!
הוּא קָדוֹשׁ
וְעַמּוֹ קָדוֹשׁ
וְכׇל־מְשָׁרְתָיו קְדוֹשִׁים
אֵין כִּקְדֻשָּׁתוֹ
וְאֵין דּוֹמֶה לּוֹ
מְקֻדָּשׁ בִּקְרוֹבָיו
וְ֝נוֹרָ֗א עַל־כׇּל־סְבִיבָֽיו
וְנַ֭עֲרָץ בְּסוֹד־קְדֹשִׁ֣ים
נִשְׂגָּב בְּרִבְבוֹת אֶרְאֶלִּים
אֵילֵי קֹֽדֶשׁ מְשׁוֹרְרִים וּמְהַלְּלִים
They are holy
and Their people is holy
and all Their camps are holy;
none is like Their holiness
and none is akin to Them!
Sanctified in those close to Them,
and awesome over all surrounding Them,
and terrific in the council of the holy,[165] Find Psalms 89:8 
awesome in the myriads of lion-angels,
and the holy divine-angels compose song and praise,
בְּשִׁיר קֹֽדֶשׁ עִירִין וְקַדִּישִׁין
רוֹעֲשִׁים וּמַקְדִּישִׁים
בְּקֹֽדֶשׁ הַקֳּדָשִׁים
עִתִּים חָשִׁים
וְעִתִּים מְמַלְלִים וְלַבְּקָרִים מִתְחַדְּשִׁים
מַקִּיפִים כֵּס הַמֶּרְכָּבָה וּקְדֻשַּׁת הַשֵּׁם מְפָרְשִׁים
וְשִׁלּוּשׁ קְדֻשָּׁה לְקָדוֹשׁ וְנוֹרָא מַקְדִּישִׁים׃
in the holy song of the holy watcher-angels,
thundering and sanctifying
in the holy of holies,
at times silent,
and at times speaking, and in mornings renewed,
surrounding the throne of the chariot and articulating the holiness of the Name,
and sanctifying the threefold holiness of the Holy and Awesome…

Notes

2 In the Hebrew, Patros From the Egyptian pꜣ-tꜣ-rsj (the land of the south), this term referred to Upper Egypt, the area of the Nile Valley south of the delta.
3 In the Hebrew, Pi-beset. Find Ezekiel 30:17
4 The last word ‘selah’ was originally a musical instruction, but in rabbinic interpretation it refers to eternity.
5 Meaning the Torah. Find Psalms 19:8

7 Note that in most Ashkenazi practice today this word is read עֲמָלֵק “in Amaleq,” almost certainly a censorship on behalf of a church well aware of Jewish usage of “Edom” as a euphemism. The next verse in the catena mentions Edom directly, but, of course, in modern Ashkenazi practice the catenae are skipped so the censorship is less obvious.
8 Found in Parma 3136 but not Berlin 1224.

11 Found in Parma 3136 but not Berlin 1224.

This is not in the Bible, which uses מלאכת עבודה to refer to work forbidden on festivals, a smaller category than work forbidden on Shabbat.
13 Abraham, who had a son at 99
14 As in, literally a tenth. The people Israel legendarily defeated seven nations in the process of conquering the land. Based on the idea that there are a total of seventy nations, Israel defeated a tenth of them.

16 Referring to Egypt. Find Genesis 10:14.

18 A small gift
19 Literally, Rahav — the primeval sea monster. Referring to Pharaoh, or Egypt in general. Find Isaiah 30:7 and Psalms 87:4
20 Referring to a beggar, who would have no money to offer as a gift.
21 As a metaphor. God would not be found among idols the same way a priest would not be found among corpses.
22 Find Proverbs 21:8

33 Referring to Daniel. Find Daniel 7 for his night vision, and Daniel 10 for his moniker “delightful man.”
34 Belshazzar — find Daniel 5:30
35 Daniel again. The lion’s den is in Daniel 6. He interprets nightmares in chapter 2.
36 Haman. Find Esther 3:1
37 Of Aḥashverosh. Find Esther 6:1
38 Zechariah 14:7
39 Another reshut, this one asking permission to insert the seder (which follows immediately afterward).
40 The seder, or azharah, is a poetic halakhic discourse on the laws of Pesaḥ. The first half, an alphabetical acrostic, discusses the laws of searching for and disposing of leaven. The second half, a name-acrostic, discusses the laws of the seder night itself. As a halakhic discourse, it features a lot of Aramaic terminology, marked here in red. Because the point of the seder is the context rather than the poetic form, the translation does not preserve the rhyme scheme. However, the acrostic is preserved in order to help with following along.

53 Pesaḥim 6a:13

90 Yerushalmi Pesaḥim 10:1:11 says that conditum fulfills the obligation for wine at the seder night. What’s conditum? A variety of spiced and sweetened white wine that was beloved in ancient Rome. The Apicius, one of the best preserved Roman-era cookbooks, has a recipe. The Tasting History Youtube channel provides a translation here, also including a thankfully scaled-down version in case you aren’t making enough for an entire villa. I tried making conditum last year and it was surprisingly good! A very nice after-dinner apertif! If you do it yourself, definitely use long pepper — it adds a slight fruitiness that regular black pepper doesn’t have. And get good wildflower honey, something local. I’m not sure what the bay leaves did — maybe spikenard would have added more. I think it might work as a vermouth substitute in a mixed drink. Here’s a picture.
